title: Home
text: A Church in Revival A Pilot Proposed Draft status: this is the working draft (DEV). Every page here is under construction and subject to change. Nothing in it is a program yet — it is a proposal, offered for prayer and honest discussion. Two questions This little book asks a church — its pastors, its elders, its small group leaders, and any member willing to read along — two questions, and it asks them honestly, not rhetorically. The first question is: do you want a revival? Not "do you like the word." Most of us do. The question is whether we want the thing itself — God moving among us in a way we do not control, at a pace we do not set, with results we cannot take credit for. Revivals in the historical record are glorious, and they are also disruptive, humbling, and costly. Churches that received them were rearranged by them. So the first movement of this book slows down on the question itself, looks hard at what revival has actually been, and gives you room to answer not yet — and to see clearly what not yet costs. The second question is: if yes, what might it take to prepare the soil? Jesus told a story about a sower whose seed fell on four kinds of ground — the hard path, the rocky ground, the thorny ground, and the good soil. The seed was the same in all four. The difference was the soil. This book takes that story seriously as a description of a congregation, and it asks: what is our soil, honestly? Are there hungry hearts here — people quietly longing for more of God than Sunday is giving them? And what would it take to till the ground — before the rain comes, not after? Where this book comes from Behind this proposal stands a longer work, Intentional Journey of the Heart , which spent years cataloging what Scripture says about spiritual cause and effect — including a hard look at how revivals rise, and why they drain away. Two convictions from that work drive everything here: A desire for more. The ordinary believer can hear God's voice in Scripture and prayer and obey it. That is not a claim for special Christians; it is the normal Christian life, and many people in ordinary pews are hungrier for it than anyone has asked them about. A recognition of the drift downward. Every revival in the record eventually cooled. The fire came from God; the cooling followed patterns — recognizable, recurring, and to a sobering degree, preventable. A church that wants revival without studying the drift is asking for a gift it is not yet prepared to keep. This book is the meeting of those two convictions: the hunger, and the honesty. The shape of the book Movement I — Do You Want a Revival? The question, taken seriously. What revival is, what it costs, and what the record shows. Movement II — What Is Your Soil? An honest diagnosis — including a simple way to discover whether your congregation holds hungry hearts, and the guardrails that keep diagnosis from becoming surveillance. Movement III — Preparing the Soil. What a church actually does, starting with its own leadership — and how to build the vessel before the rain. Movement IV — Keeping What Comes. The failure modes of previous revivals, and what keeps the wineskin supple across years and generations. Reference. The deeper material behind each movement, and pointers into Intentional Journey of the Heart for those who want the full foundations. A word before you begin No book produces a revival. God does that, and He has never once been managed into it. What a church can do — what churches in the record demonstrably did — is prepare: soften the ground, gather the hungry, form the leaders, and learn the failure modes in advance. That preparation is commanded whether or not the rain comes on our schedule: "Sow for yourselves righteousness; reap steadfast love; break up your fallow ground, for it is the time to seek the LORD, that he may come and rain righteousness upon you" (Hosea 10:12, ESV). If that verse stirs something in you, start with Start Here, and then let Movement I ask

title: The Seven Letters: What Christ Says to a Church
text: The Seven Letters: What Christ Says to a Church Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. This book opened with a question: do you want a revival? It would be fair to wonder whether that question is a device — a writer's way of getting your attention before the real material starts. It is not a device, and here is the reason. The question has already been asked. Not by an author, not by a conference speaker, not by a denominational survey — by the risen Christ, in writing, to seven real congregations, and the letters are still in your Bible. Revelation chapters 2 and 3 contain seven short letters, dictated by the risen Lord to the apostle John, each addressed to a particular church in a particular city in Roman Asia — Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, Laodicea. Real places. Real congregations, with real reputations, real pressures, and real problems, some of which they knew about and some of which they did not. These letters are the closest thing we have to hearing Christ conduct a church review. Before this book asks anything of you, it wants you to sit with what He asked of them. He writes to churches, not only to Christians The first thing to notice about the letters is easy to miss because we are so used to reading the Bible as individuals: each letter is addressed to a congregation , and it speaks to that congregation as one. "I have this against you, that you have abandoned the love you had at first" (Revelation 2:4). That sentence is not addressed to a person. It is addressed to the church at Ephesus — the whole body, as a body. In the original language the "you" is singular: Christ speaks to the congregation the way you would speak to one soul. A church, in these letters, has works. It has a reputation. It has a temperature. It has a history it is accountable for — "remember therefore from where you have fallen" — and a future that depends on what it does next. Christ treats each congregation as something that can be praised, warned, and held responsible as itself , not merely as a container full of separate believers. This should not entirely surprise us, because it is how God has always spoken. The prophets addressed whole cities and nations as moral agents — Amos read out indictments against nation after nation; Nineveh repented as a city under Jonah's preaching. Jesus did the same in His earthly ministry: He pronounced woes on Chorazin and Bethsaida as towns, and He wept over Jerusalem as one being with one will — "and you were not willing " (Matthew 23:37). The seven letters are not an oddity. They are the risen Lord continuing a practice that runs through the whole of Scripture: God deals with His people not only one by one but together — and He holds the together accountable. And yet — hold this alongside it, because the letters hold both — every one of the seven letters ends by turning to the individual. "He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches" (Revelation 2:7). Each letter closes with a promise not to the church but to "the one who conquers." The diagnosis is corporate; the response is personal. No one in Sardis could hide behind the congregation's deadness, and no one in Sardis was doomed by it either — "you have still a few names in Sardis, people who have not soiled their garments" (Revelation 3:4). A faithful person can walk in white inside a dying church. Christ never lets the church's condition override a single willing heart. That double address matters for everything this book will ask later. When we talk about the spiritual condition of a congregation, we are not inventing a category. We are standing on ground the Lord Himself staked out: churches have conditions, Christ reads them accurately, and He says so — while leaving every individual member free to hear and respond. How He examines: praise, diagnosis, prescription, promise The second thing to notice is how Christ goes about it. The seven letters are not s

title: Movement I — Do You Want a Revival?
text: Movement I — Do You Want a Revival? Draft status: movement skeleton — chapters to follow. The question sounds devotional. It is actually one of the most searching questions a church can be asked, and this movement exists to keep it from being answered too quickly. What this movement will hold: The question itself — an opening chapter that asks it pastorally and honestly: what we mean by revival, what we do not mean, and why "yes" must be allowed to cost something before it counts. What the record shows — the revival model tested against history: how genuine moves of God arose, what they did to the churches that received them, and the drift by which every one of them eventually cooled. (Standalone treatment here; full foundations in Intentional Journey of the Heart , Volume 3.) The letters to the seven churches — the risen Christ's own diagnostics of real congregations (Revelation 2–3): churches praised, churches warned, and one church that had everything but its first love. The question "do you want a revival?" turns out to be a question Christ has already asked churches directly — and He asked it of whole congregations, not just individuals. The honest "not yet" — what it means, and what it costs, to see the state of things clearly and decide to wait. This movement ends with the reader free: no manipulation, no manufactured urgency. A revival wanted for the wrong reasons is one of the failure modes Movement IV catalogs. The movement closes by handing the reader who says yes — or even I want to want it — to Movement II: before you can prepare soil, you have to know what soil you have.

title: Movement II — What Is Your Soil?
text: Movement II — What Is Your Soil? Draft status: movement skeleton — chapters to follow. Jesus' parable of the sower describes four soils — the hard path, the rocky ground, the thorny ground, and the good soil — and the difference between them decided everything the seed could do. This movement applies that description to a congregation, honestly and safely. What this movement will hold: The four soils as a congregation's self portrait — hardness, shallowness, thorns (and the parable puts riches and comfort first among the thorns), and good ground; what each looks like at the scale of a whole church, and why a congregation is not simply the average of its members. The hungry hearts question. The single most important diagnostic in this book: do we have soil that includes hearts hungry for more of God? The instrument offered here is the Four Connects — a simple, teachable way of asking how a person connects with God (and noticing when they long for more than they currently have). Used well, the Four Connects do double duty: they find the hungry hearts a congregation already contains, and they offer those same hearts a path forward. Diagnosis and invitation in one motion. The limits of counting. Why attendance, giving, and program participation cannot tell you what you need to know — the person who performs the behaviors and the person whose heart is transformed look identical to every counter. What actually reveals soil: patient observation, honest conversation, fruit over time. The guardrails. This page will be non negotiable and will sit beside every diagnostic in the book: what a church learns about its people's hearts is held for them, never over them. Mirrors for the community, never levers for management. No scores attached to money, position, or standing; no one's interior life reported upward without their knowledge and consent; and one test every leadership must run on itself — what happened to the last person who told us something we did not want to hear? The movement closes at the threshold of action: you know your soil. Movement III is about tilling it — and it starts in a place most churches do not expect.

title: When the Ground Goes Dry
text: When the Ground Goes Dry Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion, not yet a program. Here is a promise this book can make with complete confidence: if your church begins seriously praying and preparing for revival, some of you will go dry. Not might. Will. The record is unanimous. People who press in toward God — who trade a comfortable Sunday faith for real seeking — pass through seasons when prayer feels like talking to the ceiling, when the Scriptures that once glowed lie flat on the page, when the God they were just beginning to hear seems to have stepped out of the room. And here is the danger: dryness is the most misread experience in the whole spiritual life. Misread it one way, and you crush a soul God is carrying. Misread it the other way, and you comfort a soul God is calling to wake up. So this chapter exists to prevent one error above all others — the one unforgivable pastoral mistake in a preparing church: telling a dry believer that their dryness means they are disconnected from God. Say that to the wrong person and you have handed the enemy his favorite weapon, sharpened. Because dryness is not one thing. It is at least four things, nearly identical from the outside — same flat prayers, same dull Bible, same absent feelings — while calling for responses so different that two of them are exact opposites. A church that intends to pray its way toward revival must teach its leaders to tell these four apart, because instinct will not do it. Instinct says feelings gone, God gone. And instinct, at the exact moment God is doing His deepest work in a person, is usually wrong. Four kinds of dry Walk a farm in a drought and the fields all look the same: brown, cracked, still. But a farmer knows the ground goes dry for different reasons — one field was never watered, one was worked to exhaustion, one has something poisoning the soil, and one has been deliberately left fallow so it can bear more later. Same appearance; four stories; four remedies. The soul is like that. The tradition of the church — tested across centuries and, remarkably, confirmed by modern research on spiritual struggle — distinguishes four kinds of dryness: 1. Neglect — the ground nobody watered. The believer has quietly stopped using the ordinary means of grace: Scripture, prayer, worship, honest fellowship. The dryness is simple thirst, and the remedy is simple drinking. 2. Over obligation — the ground worked to death. The believer has turned faith into a crushing ledger of duties, or serves in a driven, restless overwork that has burned the joy out of everything. The remedy is not more effort; it is rest and a lighter yoke. 3. The enemy's desolation — a dry wind sent against the soul. Discouragement with a voice in it, whispering lies, pushing the believer to quit and to reverse good decisions. The remedy is to resist — hold course and refuse the counsel. 4. God's own stripping — the fallow field; what the old writers called the dark night. God Himself withdraws the felt sweetness of His presence, not because He has left, but because He is weaning the soul off the sweetness so it can learn to love Him for Himself. The remedy is to receive — wait, stay faithful, and let Him work. Look hard at the last two, because this is where the stakes live. The enemy's desolation is to be fought. God's stripping is to be welcomed. Misdiagnose, and you invert the response — you end up fighting what God is doing, or surrendering to what the enemy is doing. A leader who has only one category — all dryness is backsliding, or all dryness is a holy dark night — will eventually do real harm with the best of intentions. And one more thing must be said before the four cases, and it belongs in bold: sometimes the dryness is not spiritual at all. Depression wears the same gray coat. We will come to that squarely, because getting it wrong there is not a pastoral error but a medical one. The one question that sorts them: which way does the d

title: Movement III — Preparing the Soil
text: Movement III — Preparing the Soil Draft status: movement skeleton — chapters to follow. If Movement II found hungry hearts in your congregation, this movement is about what to do — and the order in which to do it. The order is the message. What this movement will hold: It starts with the leadership — or it does not start. The first soil to be tilled is the leadership's own. Before a church asks its members to open their hearts, its pastors and elders become a fellowship of the heart themselves: a small circle that actually practices hearing God together, tells one another the truth, and lets the Word reach their calendars and budgets and not just their doctrine. Whatever culture change that requires is the beginning of revival preparation — there is no route around it, because a congregation will not go where its leadership has not gone. Build the vessel before the rain. Revivals in the record were lost partly because the churches that received them had nowhere to put what God sent — thousands awakened, and no formed leaders to walk with them. The answer proposed here is to develop small group leadership ahead of time : a bench of ordinary members formed into companions and group leaders before they are needed. The mechanics for this exist and are already piloted — Fellowship of the Heart is the working model this book leans on, and this movement shows how a church would run that preparation as the second stage of the pilot. The practices that till. Hearing and obeying God as the one seed everything else serves; the simple practices — Scripture, listening prayer, honest fellowship — by which ground softens; and the Four Connects as the offered path for every hungry heart Movement II surfaced. When the ground goes dry. A praying church will pass through dryness, and dryness is the most misread experience in the spiritual life. This chapter gives leaders the differential — the four different things dryness can be, two of which call for opposite responses — so that a church preparing for revival does not quit at the exact moment God is doing its deepest work. The movement closes with the pilot itself: what a willing church would actually run, season by season — prepare, test, and get started.

title: The Drift Downward
text: The Drift Downward Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. The first page of this book named its two convictions in a single breath: a desire for more, and a recognition of the drift downward. Every movement so far has been unfolding the desire. This chapter unfolds the drift — because a church that wants revival without understanding the drift is asking for a gift it is not yet prepared to keep. Start with three scenes you have probably witnessed. A believer — earnest, once on fire — notices one day that her prayer has gone quiet. Not stopped, exactly. Thinned. She cannot name the month it happened. A family — church every Sunday for three generations — discovers that the grandchildren keep the building and the vocabulary but not the thing itself. Nobody rebelled. It just faded. A congregation — busy, solvent, respected — has more meetings than it had twenty years ago and less fire. The old members can name the year the temperature changed. Nobody else noticed anything change at all. Three problems, we assume. Three committees, probably. A discipleship problem, a family ministry problem, a church renewal problem — each with its own shelf of books. This chapter makes a different claim, and it is the claim on which the whole of Movement IV rests: these are not three problems. They are one law, showing up at three scales. A soul cools, a household cools, a congregation cools — by the same dynamic, for the same reason, with the same remedy. The law can be stated in one sentence: Spiritual life runs downhill the moment it stops receiving — because created life was never self sustaining. Only the Source is. If that is true, it changes how a church prepares for revival, how it reads its own decline, and above all what it must build to keep whatever God sends. So let us take it slowly. Ruin Without a Vandal The New Testament has a verse that pictures the whole thing. "Therefore we must pay much closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it" (Hebrews 2:1, ESV throughout). The word translated drift appears only here in the New Testament, and it paints a boat slipping its mooring — no storm, no leak, no hand on the rope. Just the current, and time. The drifting is passive, silent, and imperceptible while it is happening. And notice the direction built into the picture: no one drifts toward the harbor. The Old Testament paints the same law on land. The wise man walks past the field of the sluggard and finds thorns, nettles, and the stone wall broken down (Proverbs 24:30–34) — and here is the detail worth sitting with: nothing attacked that field. There is no destructive act anywhere in the account. Time plus inattention was sufficient. Ecclesiastes states it as a plain proverb of maintenance: "Through sloth the roof sinks in, and through indolence the house leaks" (Ecclesiastes 10:18). Ruin by commission needs a vandal. Ruin by omission needs only a calendar. That is the disturbing part of the drift, and also the diagnostic part: nobody has to do anything for it to happen. The believer whose prayer thinned did not decide anything. The family did not vote to lose the faith. The congregation never passed a motion to cool. Drift is what spiritual life does by default when the receiving stops — which is why it is always noticed late, and why Scripture's counsel against it comes with a dosage: "exhort one another every day, as long as it is called 'today,' that none of you may be hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" (Hebrews 3:13). Every day — because the current runs every day. Scripture even names the drift's favorite accelerant, and it is not persecution. It is prosperity. "Take care lest you forget the LORD your God... lest, when you have eaten and are full... then your heart be lifted up, and you forget the LORD your God" (Deuteronomy 8:11–14). Hard times drive a people to their knees; the full stomach quietly closes the valve. Notice, all through Deuteronomy, that remember is a

title: Handing the Fire
text: Handing the Fire Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Suppose the rain comes. Suppose God answers the prayers this book has been teaching you to pray — the soil softens, the fire falls, and your church is rearranged by a mercy it did not manufacture. Everything in this movement so far has been about keeping that fire through the ordinary drift of years. But one test remains, and it is the hardest, because it does not arrive for twenty years and cannot be crammed for the night before. The ultimate survival test of a revival is not the next budget year. It is the next generation. The record is unsentimental here. Revival's children inherit one of two things: living fire, or a photograph of one. They grow up either warming their hands at something real, or dusting a frame on the mantel — the building their grandparents built, the hymns their grandparents wept through, the vocabulary of an encounter they never had. And the difference between those inheritances is not luck. It follows patterns, and — this is the mercy in it — much of what matters can be built now, before any rain falls. This chapter is written for elders, yes. But just as much for parents and grandparents, because everything the research says points to one uncomfortable, liberating fact: the handoff does not happen in the sanctuary. It happens at your table. One Generation from Forgetting Start with the most sobering sentence in the book of Judges. Joshua's generation had seen it all — the sea opened, the Jordan cut off, the walls down. If any generation ever had a revival to hand down, it was this one. And then: "And all that generation also were gathered to their fathers. And there arose another generation after them who did not know the LORD or the work that he had done for Israel" (Judges 2:10, ESV). One generation. Not a slow slide across centuries — one funeral cycle between the greatest move of God in Israel's history and children who did not know the LORD . And before we blame distance, Scripture closes that exit too. Eli's sons were priests' sons, raised inside the sanctuary, handling the offerings of God with their own hands: "They did not know the LORD" (1 Samuel 2:12, ESV). Even young Samuel, sleeping steps from the ark, "did not yet know the LORD" (1 Samuel 3:7, ESV) — not until the night God called him by name and he answered for himself. There is the whole problem in three verses. Proximity to the things of God is not knowledge of God. You can raise a child inside the revival, and the child can still miss the LORD of it. Why? Because of something Scripture teaches plainly and modern research has spent fifty years confirming from the outside. What No Parent Can Hand Down Faith is not heritable. John's Gospel says it with three hammer blows: the children of God are born "not of blood nor of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God" (John 1:13, ESV). Not of blood — bloodline gets you nothing. Not of the will of the flesh, not of the will of man — no father's determination, however fierce, can will a child into the new birth. Only God. Ezekiel says it from another angle. Israel had a proverb — the fathers eat sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge — and God abolished it: "The son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father, nor the father suffer for the iniquity of the son" (Ezekiel 18:20, ESV). Guilt does not run in the blood — and, the same chapter's quieter corollary, neither does righteousness. Each soul stands before God on its own feet. And Jeremiah puts the end of hereditary religion inside the new covenant promise itself: "they shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest" (Jeremiah 31:34, ESV). All of them — firsthand. Nobody knows God by being somebody's child. An old saying, often credited to Corrie ten Boom, puts it in five words: God has no grandchildren. The sociologists, with their decades long surveys, hit the same wall. Nobody can be born a volunteer

title: Movement IV — Keeping What Comes
text: Movement IV — Keeping What Comes Draft status: movement skeleton — chapters to follow. Every revival in the historical record cooled. That single fact shapes this whole movement — because the cooling was not random. It followed patterns, the patterns are describable, and a church that studies them before the rain has a chance the churches of the record did not take. What this movement will hold: The drift downward, named. The spine of this book: what the longer work behind it calls drift — the universal tendency of spiritual life, in a soul and in a church, to run downhill when it stops receiving. Why the fire fades by default; why maintaining yesterday's blessing takes today's obedience; why no church coasts upward. The failure modes of previous revivals. The recurring ways moves of God were lost: the form outliving the power (structures kept polishing themselves after the life had gone); success manufacturing the comfort and riches that choke the next generation (the thorns grow from the harvest); the movement housed in one leader who could not be told hard truth and could not be succeeded; the hungry converted and never formed. Each failure mode is paired with the preparation that answers it — most of which this book has already walked you through, which is the point. The wineskin. Jesus' warning that new wine bursts old wineskins, taken seriously as a law of institutions: every fresh work routinizes, and what keeps a wineskin supple is designed renewal — including a willingness to let structures (even this pilot's structures) be examined, refreshed, and if need be, retired. Handing the fire to the next generation. Faith is not heritable; the conditions of faith are. What the record and the research say about how revival's children either inherit living fire or a photograph of one — warmth as the carrier, honest questions welcomed rather than managed, and a household and church culture that provokes the next generation to ask for the real thing. The movement — and the book — closes where it began: with the two questions, now fully informed. Do you want a revival? And now that you know what the soil requires: will you begin?

title: Reference
text: Reference Draft status: section skeleton — material to follow. This section holds the deeper material behind the four movements, for readers — especially leaders — who want to see the foundations. What this section will hold: The research behind each movement — standalone treatments of the revival record, the seven churches diagnostics, the desolation differential, the drift and decay dynamics, and formation across generations, each readable on its own and each pointing into Intentional Journey of the Heart for the full scholarly base. How these foundations were tested. Before this proposal was assembled, the twelve foundation documents behind it were each placed before an independent review panel — voices from measurement science, pastoral practice, clinical trauma care, missiology, and church history, asked to challenge the basic assumptions. A summary chapter will report what held (the core conviction that ordinary believers can hear and obey God survived all twelve reviews) and what the reviewers demanded (most of the guardrails in Movement II exist because reviewers insisted on them). The reviewers' insights, preserved. The individual review findings were too valuable to lose to summary — the ones that shaped this book will be kept here as reference reading, credited to the perspective that produced them. Pointers and further reading — where each thread continues in Intentional Journey of the Heart and in the Fellowship of the Heart pilot materials.

title: Start here
text: Start Here Draft status: working draft (DEV) — structure and content still forming. Who this book is for It is written for a church — first for its leadership (pastors, elders, and those who carry responsibility for the congregation's direction), and then for its small group leaders and any member who wants to read along. You do not need any background in the larger body of work this proposal draws from. Where deeper foundations exist, the book points to them; it never requires them. What this book is — and is not It is a proposal for a pilot. Not a program to install, but a path a willing church could walk: an honest season of asking whether we want revival, discovering the state of our soil, preparing it, and learning — in advance — how churches before us received a move of God and then lost it. It is not a technique for producing revival. No such technique exists. The whole record of the church says the fire falls where God wills. The same record says, just as clearly, that the falling fire found some houses ready and some not — and that readiness had a describable shape. How the book is organized Four movements, in order, each building on the one before: 1. Do You Want a Revival? — the question itself, with the cost on the table. 2. What Is Your Soil? — diagnosis before cultivation, with strong guardrails. 3. Preparing the Soil — the practices, beginning with the leadership's own hearts. 4. Keeping What Comes — sustainment, and the failure modes of the past. Then a Reference section holding the deeper material: the research behind each movement, a summary of how these foundations were tested, and pointers into Intentional Journey of the Heart — the reference base this book stands on — and Fellowship of the Heart , the small group pilot this book will lean on for its leadership formation mechanics. The order matters One conviction shapes the sequence of this book more than any other: preparation starts at the top. A congregation's soil is tilled from the leadership downward — a leadership that has itself become a fellowship of the heart, before it asks anyone else to become one. Movement III is built around that order, and it is the hardest and most important thing in this proposal. Reading time The four movements are deliberately short enough for a leadership team to read together across a handful of meetings, one movement at a time, with discussion. The Reference section is there for whoever wants to go further.
